19	// Package resolver defines APIs for name resolution in gRPC.
20	// All APIs in this package are experimental.
21	package resolver
22	
23	import (
24		"context"
25		"errors"
26		"fmt"
27		"net"
28		"net/url"
29		"strings"
30	
31		"google.golang.org/grpc/attributes"
32		"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials"
33		"google.golang.org/grpc/experimental/stats"
34		"google.golang.org/grpc/internal"
35		"google.golang.org/grpc/serviceconfig"
36	)
37	
38	var (
39		// m is a map from scheme to resolver builder.
40		m = make(map[string]Builder)
41		// defaultScheme is the default scheme to use.
42		defaultScheme = "passthrough"
43	)
44	
45	// TODO(bar) install dns resolver in init(){}.
46	
47	// Register registers the resolver builder to the resolver map. b.Scheme will
48	// be used as the scheme registered with this builder. The registry is case
49	// sensitive, and schemes should not contain any uppercase characters.
50	//
51	// NOTE: this function must only be called during initialization time (i.e. in
52	// an init() function), and is not thread-safe. If multiple Resolvers are
53	// registered with the same name, the one registered last will take effect.
54	func Register(b Builder) {
55		m[b.Scheme()] = b
56	}
57	
58	// Get returns the resolver builder registered with the given scheme.
59	//
60	// If no builder is register with the scheme, nil will be returned.
61	func Get(scheme string) Builder {
62		if b, ok := m[scheme]; ok {
63			return b
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// SetDefaultScheme sets the default scheme that will be used. The default
69	// scheme is initially set to "passthrough".
70	//
71	// NOTE: this function must only be called during initialization time (i.e. in
72	// an init() function), and is not thread-safe. The scheme set last overrides
73	// previously set values.
74	func SetDefaultScheme(scheme string) {
75		defaultScheme = scheme
76		internal.UserSetDefaultScheme = true
77	}
78	
79	// GetDefaultScheme gets the default scheme that will be used by grpc.Dial.  If
80	// SetDefaultScheme is never called, the default scheme used by grpc.NewClient is "dns" instead.
81	func GetDefaultScheme() string {
82		return defaultScheme
83	}
84	
85	// Address represents a server the client connects to.
86	//
87	// # Experimental
88	//
89	// Notice: This type is EXPERIMENTAL and may be changed or removed in a
90	// later release.
91	type Address struct {
92		// Addr is the server address on which a connection will be established.
93		Addr string
94	
95		// ServerName is the name of this address.
96		// If non-empty, the ServerName is used as the transport certification authority for
97		// the address, instead of the hostname from the Dial target string. In most cases,
98		// this should not be set.
99		//
100		// WARNING: ServerName must only be populated with trusted values. It
101		// is insecure to populate it with data from untrusted inputs since untrusted
102		// values could be used to bypass the authority checks performed by TLS.
103		ServerName string
104	
105		// Attributes contains arbitrary data about this address intended for
106		// consumption by the SubConn.
107		Attributes *attributes.Attributes
108	
109		// BalancerAttributes contains arbitrary data about this address intended
110		// for consumption by the LB policy.  These attributes do not affect SubConn
111		// creation, connection establishment, handshaking, etc.
112		//
113		// Deprecated: when an Address is inside an Endpoint, this field should not
114		// be used, and it will eventually be removed entirely.
115		BalancerAttributes *attributes.Attributes
116	
117		// Metadata is the information associated with Addr, which may be used
118		// to make load balancing decision.
119		//
120		// Deprecated: use Attributes instead.
121		Metadata any
122	}
123	
124	// Equal returns whether a and o are identical.  Metadata is compared directly,
125	// not with any recursive introspection.
126	//
127	// This method compares all fields of the address. When used to tell apart
128	// addresses during subchannel creation or connection establishment, it might be
129	// more appropriate for the caller to implement custom equality logic.
130	func (a Address) Equal(o Address) bool {
131		return a.Addr == o.Addr && a.ServerName == o.ServerName &&
132			a.Attributes.Equal(o.Attributes) &&
133			a.BalancerAttributes.Equal(o.BalancerAttributes) &&
134			a.Metadata == o.Metadata
135	}
136	
137	// String returns JSON formatted string representation of the address.
138	func (a Address) String() string {
139		var sb strings.Builder
140		sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("{Addr: %q, ", a.Addr))
141		sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("ServerName: %q, ", a.ServerName))
142		if a.Attributes != nil {
143			sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("Attributes: %v, ", a.Attributes.String()))
144		}
145		if a.BalancerAttributes != nil {
146			sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("BalancerAttributes: %v", a.BalancerAttributes.String()))
147		}
148		sb.WriteString("}")
149		return sb.String()
150	}
151	
152	// BuildOptions includes additional information for the builder to create
153	// the resolver.
154	type BuildOptions struct {
155		// DisableServiceConfig indicates whether a resolver implementation should
156		// fetch service config data.
157		DisableServiceConfig bool
158		// DialCreds is the transport credentials used by the ClientConn for
159		// communicating with the target gRPC service (set via
160		// WithTransportCredentials). In cases where a name resolution service
161		// requires the same credentials, the resolver may use this field. In most
162		// cases though, it is not appropriate, and this field may be ignored.
163		DialCreds credentials.TransportCredentials
164		// CredsBundle is the credentials bundle used by the ClientConn for
165		// communicating with the target gRPC service (set via
166		// WithCredentialsBundle). In cases where a name resolution service
167		// requires the same credentials, the resolver may use this field. In most
168		// cases though, it is not appropriate, and this field may be ignored.
169		CredsBundle credentials.Bundle
170		// Dialer is the custom dialer used by the ClientConn for dialling the
171		// target gRPC service (set via WithDialer). In cases where a name
172		// resolution service requires the same dialer, the resolver may use this
173		// field. In most cases though, it is not appropriate, and this field may
174		// be ignored.
175		Dialer func(context.Context, string) (net.Conn, error)
176		// Authority is the effective authority of the clientconn for which the
177		// resolver is built.
178		Authority string
179		// MetricsRecorder is the metrics recorder to do recording.
180		MetricsRecorder stats.MetricsRecorder
181	}
182	
183	// An Endpoint is one network endpoint, or server, which may have multiple
184	// addresses with which it can be accessed.
185	// TODO(i/8773) : make resolver.Endpoint and resolver.Address immutable
186	type Endpoint struct {
187		// Addresses contains a list of addresses used to access this endpoint.
188		Addresses []Address
189	
190		// Attributes contains arbitrary data about this endpoint intended for
191		// consumption by the LB policy.
192		Attributes *attributes.Attributes
193	}
194	
195	// State contains the current Resolver state relevant to the ClientConn.
196	type State struct {
197		// Addresses is the latest set of resolved addresses for the target.
198		//
199		// If a resolver sets Addresses but does not set Endpoints, one Endpoint
200		// will be created for each Address before the State is passed to the LB
201		// policy.  The BalancerAttributes of each entry in Addresses will be set
202		// in Endpoints.Attributes, and be cleared in the Endpoint's Address's
203		// BalancerAttributes.
204		//
205		// Soon, Addresses will be deprecated and replaced fully by Endpoints.
206		Addresses []Address
207	
208		// Endpoints is the latest set of resolved endpoints for the target.
209		//
210		// If a resolver produces a State containing Endpoints but not Addresses,
211		// it must take care to ensure the LB policies it selects will support
212		// Endpoints.
213		Endpoints []Endpoint
214	
215		// ServiceConfig contains the result from parsing the latest service
216		// config.  If it is nil, it indicates no service config is present or the
217		// resolver does not provide service configs.
218		ServiceConfig *serviceconfig.ParseResult
219	
220		// Attributes contains arbitrary data about the resolver intended for
221		// consumption by the load balancing policy.
222		Attributes *attributes.Attributes
223	}
224	
225	// ClientConn contains the callbacks for resolver to notify any updates
226	// to the gRPC ClientConn.
227	//
228	// This interface is to be implemented by gRPC. Users should not need a
229	// brand new implementation of this interface. For the situations like
230	// testing, the new implementation should embed this interface. This allows
231	// gRPC to add new methods to this interface.
232	type ClientConn interface {
233		// UpdateState updates the state of the ClientConn appropriately.
234		//
235		// If an error is returned, the resolver should try to resolve the
236		// target again. The resolver should use a backoff timer to prevent
237		// overloading the server with requests. If a resolver is certain that
238		// reresolving will not change the result, e.g. because it is
239		// a watch-based resolver, returned errors can be ignored.
240		//
241		// If the resolved State is the same as the last reported one, calling
242		// UpdateState can be omitted.
243		UpdateState(State) error
244		// ReportError notifies the ClientConn that the Resolver encountered an
245		// error. The ClientConn then forwards this error to the load balancing
246		// policy.
247		ReportError(error)
248		// NewAddress is called by resolver to notify ClientConn a new list
249		// of resolved addresses.
250		// The address list should be the complete list of resolved addresses.
251		//
252		// Deprecated: Use UpdateState instead.
253		NewAddress(addresses []Address)
254		// ParseServiceConfig parses the provided service config and returns an
255		// object that provides the parsed config.
256		ParseServiceConfig(serviceConfigJSON string) *serviceconfig.ParseResult
257	}
258	
259	// Target represents a target for gRPC, as specified in:
260	// https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/master/doc/naming.md.
261	// It is parsed from the target string that gets passed into Dial or DialContext
262	// by the user. And gRPC passes it to the resolver and the balancer.
263	//
264	// If the target follows the naming spec, and the parsed scheme is registered
265	// with gRPC, we will parse the target string according to the spec. If the
266	// target does not contain a scheme or if the parsed scheme is not registered
267	// (i.e. no corresponding resolver available to resolve the endpoint), we will
268	// apply the default scheme, and will attempt to reparse it.
269	type Target struct {
270		// URL contains the parsed dial target with an optional default scheme added
271		// to it if the original dial target contained no scheme or contained an
272		// unregistered scheme. Any query params specified in the original dial
273		// target can be accessed from here.
274		URL url.URL
275	}
276	
277	// Endpoint retrieves endpoint without leading "/" from either `URL.Path`
278	// or `URL.Opaque`. The latter is used when the former is empty.
279	func (t Target) Endpoint() string {
280		endpoint := t.URL.Path
281		if endpoint == "" {
282			endpoint = t.URL.Opaque
283		}
284		// For targets of the form "[scheme]://[authority]/endpoint, the endpoint
285		// value returned from url.Parse() contains a leading "/". Although this is
286		// in accordance with RFC 3986, we do not want to break existing resolver
287		// implementations which expect the endpoint without the leading "/". So, we
288		// end up stripping the leading "/" here. But this will result in an
289		// incorrect parsing for something like "unix:///path/to/socket". Since we
290		// own the "unix" resolver, we can workaround in the unix resolver by using
291		// the `URL` field.
292		return strings.TrimPrefix(endpoint, "/")
293	}
294	
295	// String returns the canonical string representation of Target.
296	func (t Target) String() string {
297		return t.URL.Scheme + "://" + t.URL.Host + "/" + t.Endpoint()
298	}
299	
300	// Builder creates a resolver that will be used to watch name resolution updates.
301	type Builder interface {
302		// Build creates a new resolver for the given target.
303		//
304		// gRPC dial calls Build synchronously, and fails if the returned error is
305		// not nil.
306		Build(target Target, cc ClientConn, opts BuildOptions) (Resolver, error)
307		// Scheme returns the scheme supported by this resolver.  Scheme is defined
308		// at https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/master/doc/naming.md.  The returned
309		// string should not contain uppercase characters, as they will not match
310		// the parsed target's scheme as defined in RFC 3986.
311		Scheme() string
312	}
313	
314	// ResolveNowOptions includes additional information for ResolveNow.
315	type ResolveNowOptions struct{}
316	
317	// Resolver watches for the updates on the specified target.
318	// Updates include address updates and service config updates.
319	type Resolver interface {
320		// ResolveNow will be called by gRPC to try to resolve the target name
321		// again. It's just a hint, resolver can ignore this if it's not necessary.
322		//
323		// It could be called multiple times concurrently.
324		ResolveNow(ResolveNowOptions)
325		// Close closes the resolver.
326		Close()
327	}
328	
329	// AuthorityOverrider is implemented by Builders that wish to override the
330	// default authority for the ClientConn.
331	// By default, the authority used is target.Endpoint().
332	type AuthorityOverrider interface {
333		// OverrideAuthority returns the authority to use for a ClientConn with the
334		// given target. The implementation must generate it without blocking,
335		// typically in line, and must keep it unchanged.
336		//
337		// The returned string must be a valid ":authority" header value, i.e. be
338		// encoded according to
339		// [RFC3986](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3986#section-3.2) as
340		// necessary.
341		OverrideAuthority(Target) string
342	}
343	
344	// ValidateEndpoints validates endpoints from a petiole policy's perspective.
345	// Petiole policies should call this before calling into their children. See
346	// [gRPC A61](https://github.com/grpc/proposal/blob/master/A61-IPv4-IPv6-dualstack-backends.md)
347	// for details.
348	func ValidateEndpoints(endpoints []Endpoint) error {
349		if len(endpoints) == 0 {
350			return errors.New("endpoints list is empty")
351		}
352	
353		for _, endpoint := range endpoints {
354			for range endpoint.Addresses {
355				return nil
356			}
357		}
358		return errors.New("endpoints list contains no addresses")
359	}

content.text: This is a substantial exhaustive audit. Let me identify the carrier types first, then search production code systematically. The metadata values are `Address.Attributes`, `Address.BalancerAttributes`, `Address.Metadata`, and `Endpoint.Attributes`. Their carriers are `resolver.Address`, `resolver.Endpoint`, `resolver.State`, and `balancer.SubConn` (interface — the easy-to-miss kind).

I now have a complete picture. Here is the exhaustive audit.

# Audit: production code retaining per-address / per-endpoint metadata

**The metadata values** are `Address.Attributes`, `Address.BalancerAttributes`, `Address.Metadata` (`resolver/resolver.go:107-121`) and `Endpoint.Attributes` (`resolver/resolver.go:192`). **Carriers** that transport them: `resolver.Address`, `resolver.Endpoint`, `resolver.State`, `balancer.ClientConnState.ResolverState`, and — the easy-to-miss interface — `balancer.SubConn` (a SubConn permanently retains the `[]resolver.Address` it was created with, via `addrConn.addrs`; see area 1). Any struct/map/cache keeping one of these alive across calls retains metadata. `balancer.State` retains a `Picker`, and pickers retain SubConns — so caching a `balancer.State` is an *indirect* retainer (flagged below).

Excludes `_test.go`, `examples/`, `interop/`, `internal/testutils/`, `benchmark/`.

---

## 1. Core ClientConn & transport (`google.golang.org/grpc` root)

- `clientconn.go:1263` — `addrConn.addrs []resolver.Address` — the full resolved address list the LB policy passed to `NewSubConn`/`updateAddrs`. **This is the root SubConn→metadata retention**: every interface-typed `balancer.SubConn` holder below ultimately keeps these alive.
- `clientconn.go:1262` — `addrConn.curAddr resolver.Address` — the single address currently dialed, copied from `addrs`.
- `balancer_wrapper.go:269` — `acBalancerWrapper` wraps `*addrConn`; it *is* the `balancer.SubConn` handed to policies, so it transitively retains `addrConn.addrs`.
- `internal/transport/http2_client.go:80` — `http2Client.address resolver.Address` — the address used to establish the transport, kept for `:authority`/handshake for the connection's lifetime.
- `resolver_wrapper.go:49` — `ccBalancerWrapper`'s resolver side: `ccResolverWrapper.curState resolver.State` — the last state from the resolver (Addresses + Endpoints + Attributes), cached for diffing/reprocessing.

## 2. Balancer infrastructure (internal)

- `internal/balancergroup/balancergroup.go:227` — `scToSubBalancer map[balancer.SubConn]*subBalancerWrapper` — **interface key**; keys are SubConns → retains their addresses.
- `internal/balancergroup/balancergroup.go:58` — `subBalancerWrapper.state balancer.State` — *indirect*: cached picker retains SubConns.
- `internal/balancer/gracefulswitch/gracefulswitch.go:302` — `balancerWrapper.subconns map[balancer.SubConn]bool` — **interface key**; SubConns created by the wrapped balancer.
- `internal/balancer/gracefulswitch/gracefulswitch.go:301` — `balancerWrapper.lastState balancer.State` — *indirect* via cached picker.

## 3. Base & endpoint balancers (`balancer/`)

- `balancer/base/balancer.go:68` — `baseBalancer.subConns *resolver.AddressMapV2[balancer.SubConn]` — keyed by address; both the key (Addr/ServerName/Attributes) and the value SubConn retain metadata.
- `balancer/base/balancer.go:69` — `baseBalancer.scStates map[balancer.SubConn]connectivity.State` — **interface key**.
- `balancer/base/base.go:49,55` — `PickerBuildInfo.ReadySCs map[balancer.SubConn]SubConnInfo`, and `SubConnInfo.Address resolver.Address` — the address is explicitly stored beside each ready SubConn and flows into pickers.
- `balancer/pickfirst/pickfirst.go:201` — `pickfirstBalancer.subConns *resolver.AddressMapV2[*scData]` — keyed by address.
- `balancer/pickfirst/pickfirst.go:156-157` — `scData.subConn balancer.SubConn` + `scData.addr resolver.Address` — per-SubConn record retaining its address.
- `balancer/pickfirst/pickfirst.go:877` — `addressList.addresses []resolver.Address` — flattened endpoint address list retained across pick iterations (held by `scData`/picker).
- `balancer/weightedroundrobin/balancer.go:219` — `addressWeights *resolver.AddressMapV2[*endpointWeight]` — keyed by address.
- `balancer/weightedroundrobin/balancer.go:220` — `endpointToWeight *resolver.EndpointMap[*endpointWeight]` — keyed by endpoint (endpoint addresses).
- `balancer/weightedroundrobin/balancer.go:221` — `scToWeight map[balancer.SubConn]*endpointWeight` — **interface key**.
- `balancer/weightedroundrobin/balancer.go:532` — `endpointWeight.pickedSC balancer.SubConn` — retained SubConn per weight entry.
- `balancer/leastrequest/leastrequest.go:110` — `leastRequestBalancer.endpointRPCCounts *resolver.EndpointMap[*atomic.Int32]` — keyed by endpoint.
- `balancer/ringhash/ringhash.go:101` — `ringhashBalancer.endpointStates *resolver.EndpointMap[*endpointState]` — keyed by endpoint.
- `balancer/endpointsharding/endpointsharding.go:103` — `endpointSharding.children atomic.Pointer[resolver.EndpointMap[*balancerWrapper]]` — keyed by endpoint.
- `balancer/endpointsharding/endpointsharding.go:44-45` — `ChildState.Endpoint resolver.Endpoint` (held by `balancerWrapper.childState`, line 347, and surfaced through `pickerWithChildStates.childStates`, line 311) — the endpoint (Attributes + addresses) is retained per child and exposed to parents/pickers.

## 4. grpclb (`balancer/grpclb/`)

- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb.go:226` — `lbBalancer.subConns map[resolver.Address]balancer.SubConn` — **address key + interface value**.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb.go:227` — `lbBalancer.scStates map[balancer.SubConn]connectivity.State` — **interface key**.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb.go:218,223,237` — `backendAddrs`, `backendAddrsWithoutMetadata`, `resolvedBackendAddrs []resolver.Address` — retained backend lists (note: `backendAddrsWithoutMetadata` deliberately nils `Metadata`, so it retains Addr/Attributes only).
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb_util.go:44` — `lbCacheClientConn.subConnToAddr map[balancer.SubConn]resolver.Address` — **interface key**; value strips `Attributes` (`addrWithoutAttrs.Attributes = nil`, line ~68), so a *partial* metadata retainer worth noting during the rework.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb_util.go:47` — `subConnCache map[resolver.Address]*subConnCacheEntry` with `subConnCacheEntry.sc balancer.SubConn` (line 48) — cached deleted SubConns keyed by (attribute-stripped) address.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb_util.go:95` — `lbCacheSubConn` embeds `balancer.SubConn` — wrapper transitively retains addresses.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb_picker.go:108,140` — `rrPicker.subConns` / `lbPicker.subConns []balancer.SubConn` — **interface slice** snapshots of READY SubConns held by pickers.
- `balancer/grpclb/state/state.go:36` — `State.BalancerAddresses []resolver.Address` — grpclb balancer addresses stashed in resolver-state attributes.
- `balancer/grpclb/grpclb_remote_balancer.go:143-144` — `scKey resolver.Address` / `sc balancer.SubConn` (fields of the local aggregation struct) — retained during remote-balancer processing.

## 5. RLS (`balancer/rls/`)

- `balancer/rls/balancer.go:193-194` — `rlsBalancer.childPolicies map[string]*childPolicyWrapper` and `defaultPolicy` — each child policy wrapper is fed and retains a `resolver.State` (see next).
- `balancer/rls/balancer.go:190` — `rlsBalancer.resolverState resolver.State` — cached resolver state (Addresses/Endpoints/Attributes) replayed to child policies.
- `balancer/rls/cache.go:47` — `cacheEntry.childPolicyWrappers []*childPolicyWrapper` — data-cache entries retain child-policy wrappers (→ their resolver state) across RPCs, with TTL/eviction.

## 6. Resolvers

- `resolver/manual/manual.go:68` — `Resolver.lastSeenState *resolver.State` — manual resolver caches the last reported state.
- `internal/resolver/delegatingresolver/delegatingresolver.go:61` — `delegatingResolver.targetResolverState *resolver.State` — cached target-resolver state, recombined with proxy addresses on each update.
- `internal/resolver/delegatingresolver/delegatingresolver.go:62` — `delegatingResolver.proxyAddrs []resolver.Address` — retained resolved proxy addresses.

## 7. xds balancers (`internal/xds/balancer/`)

- `internal/xds/balancer/clusterimpl/clusterimpl.go:564` — `scWrapper` embeds `balancer.SubConn` (plus derived `localityID`/`hostname`) — **interface embed**; transitively retains SubConn addresses.
- `internal/xds/balancer/outlierdetection/subconn_wrapper.go:34` — `subConnWrapper` embeds `balancer.SubConn`.
- `internal/xds/balancer/outlierdetection/subconn_wrapper.go:55` — `subConnWrapper.addresses []resolver.Address` — the address(es) the SubConn was created with, kept for address-change handling.
- `internal/xds/balancer/outlierdetection/subconn_wrapper.go:58` — `subConnWrapper.latestHealthState balancer.SubConnState` — cached SubConn state (carrier; retained for unejection).
- `internal/xds/balancer/outlierdetection/balancer.go:221` — `outlierDetectionBalancer.endpoints *resolver.EndpointMap[*endpointInfo]` — keyed by endpoint; `endpointInfo` also holds the wrapped SubConns.
- `internal/xds/balancer/outlierdetection/balancer.go:161,177` — `state balancer.SubConnState` fields on the internal update structs — retained on the work-serializer buffer.
- `internal/xds/balancer/priority/balancer_child.go:42` — `childBalancer.rState resolver.State` — cached per-priority child resolver state (replayed on start/config change).
- `internal/xds/balancer/priority/balancer_child.go:51` — `childBalancer.state balancer.State` — *indirect* via cached picker.

## 8. xds resolver / client resources (`internal/xds/xdsclient/`)

- `internal/xds/xdsclient/xdsresource/xdsconfig.go:97` — `EndpointsUpdate/config field Endpoints []resolver.Endpoint` — parsed EDS endpoints (with Attributes) retained in the xds config object.
- `internal/xds/xdsclient/xdsresource/type_eds.go:53` — `Endpoint.ResolverEndpoint resolver.Endpoint` — each EDS endpoint keeps a materialized `resolver.Endpoint` (with locality/weight Attributes).

## 9. `resolver` map infrastructure (the shared retainer, `resolver/map.go`)

Every `AddressMapV2`/`EndpointMap` above stores carriers internally, so these are the shared mechanism the rework must treat carefully:
- `resolver/map.go:29` — `addressMapEntry.addr Address` — stores the **full** Address (incl. Attributes) as the entry, even though the map *key* (`toMapKey`, line 60) drops Attributes/BalancerAttributes/Metadata. Equality is done via `Attributes.Equal` at line 84 — the exact comparison the rework changes.
- `resolver/map.go:188` — `endpointData.decodedKey Endpoint` — stores an `Endpoint{Addresses: e.Addresses}` (drops `Endpoint.Attributes`, keeps addresses incl. their per-address Attributes) keyed only on the unordered address-string set (`encodeEndpoint`, line 201).

---

### Rework flags (where equality/lifetime semantics actually bite)
- **Interface-typed holders that no text search for `attributes`/`resolver.Address` reaches**: every `map[balancer.SubConn]…` and `[]balancer.SubConn` in areas 2–7, and the `balancer.SubConn` embeds in clusterimpl/outlierdetection/grpclb — all retain addresses only through the SubConn interface.
- **Partial retainers** (already strip some metadata today — verify the rework keeps them consistent): grpclb `subConnToAddr`/`subConnCache` (nils Attributes), grpclb `backendAddrsWithoutMetadata` (nils Metadata), and `AddressMapV2` (drops non-Attributes fields from the key but keeps them in the entry).
- **Indirect via `balancer.State`/`Picker`**: `gracefulswitch.lastState`, `balancergroup.subBalancerWrapper.state`, `priority.childBalancer.state`, `endpointsharding.ChildState.State` — these hold pickers that hold SubConns; equality changes propagate through them.

### Rework flags (where equality/lifetime semantics actually bite)
- **Interface-typed holders that no text search for `attributes`/`resolver.Address` reaches**: every `map[balancer.SubConn]…` and `[]balancer.SubConn` in areas 2–7, and the `balancer.SubConn` embeds in clusterimpl/outlierdetection/grpclb — all retain addresses only through the SubConn interface.
- **Partial retainers** (already strip some metadata today — verify the rework keeps them consistent): grpclb `subConnToAddr`/`subConnCache` (nils Attributes), grpclb `backendAddrsWithoutMetadata` (nils Metadata), and `AddressMapV2` (drops non-Attributes fields from the key but keeps them in the entry).
- **Indirect via `balancer.State`/`Picker`**: `gracefulswitch.lastState`, `balancergroup.subBalancerWrapper.state`, `priority.childBalancer.state`, `endpointsharding.ChildState.State` — these hold pickers that hold SubConns; equality changes propagate through them.
